figures.caption: Table 1. Comparative performance on the SIICU dataset. Values denote mean ± standard deviation across four independent runs. Given the extreme label sparsity, evaluation prioritizes AUPRC over AUROC to mitigate the impact of class imbalance. MATA-Former achieving decisive gains in precision-oriented metrics (P@1, P@5) and demonstrating superior sensitivity to rare, high-priority clinical risks.
Table 7. Cohort selection criteria and experimental design for the three downstream clinical prediction tasks derived from the MIMIC-IV database.

Figure 3. Ablation and mechanism analysis. (a) Additive bias superiorly preserves semantic integrity compared to temporal encoding. (b)-(c) MSE maximizes AUPRC by maintaining a distinct signal-noise separation valley. (d) Decoupling µ and α achieves optimality in capturing heterogeneous clinical dynamics.

Figure 9. Sensitivity Analysis of Label Binarization Threshold (τ ). The figure illustrates the model’s performance stability across a wide range of risk severity definitions (τ ∈ [0.3, 0.9]). While Precision@1 naturally decays as the threshold broadens, the ranking metrics (Global and Sample-wise AUPRC) exhibit negligible fluctuation (∆ < 0.013). This threshold invariance confirms that MATA-Former robustly captures the continuous progression of risk rather than overfitting to a specific binary cutoff.

Figure 10. Ablation Study on Focal Loss Hyperparameters. To investigate the efficacy of handling zero-inflated data via Focal Loss, we conducted a grid search over the focusing parameter γ ∈ {1.0, 2.0, 4.0} and the balancing factor α. The results demonstrate significant performance volatility and a failure to consistently surpass the unweighted MSE baseline across various configurations. This empirical evidence supports the adoption of unweighted MSE as the superior objective function for learning continuous clinical risk trajectories.

Figure 5. Visualization of bias parameters. (Top) No-Slope setting; (Middle) No-Peak setting; (Bottom) Full model configuration. The columns represent µ Distribution, α Distribution, and Joint Distribution respectively.

Figure 1. Statistics of SIICU. (a) Heavy-tailed distribution of sequence lengths. (b) Irregular time intervals. (c) Density of positive sample ratios. (d) Long-tail distribution of risks. These distributions highlight the dataset’s high heterogeneity and sparsity.
Table 6. Detailed Events and Medical History Records.

Figure 7. Visualization of Multi-Head Attention Patterns in Layer 0. The figure displays the attention weight matrices for all 32 heads. The heatmaps illustrate the strictly causal structure (lower triangular mask) and the diversity of attention distributions, where varying degrees of concentration indicate different effective receptive fields learned by the MATA mechanism.

Figure 4. Visualization of learnable temporal dynamics. (a) Dynamic Parameter Evolution: The distributional shift of Laplacian parameters (µ, α) from Layer 0 to 5 reveals a transition from discrete temporal filtering to high-level semantic abstraction. (b) Aggregated Attention Alignment: To accommodate extended clinical trajectories, the heatmap utilizes 30 × 30 block-wise average pooling. The resulting vertical strata demonstrate consistent anchoring on pivotal prognostic determinants—specifically abnormal labs and interventional history—validating the structural alignment between the Laplacian prior and clinical causality.

Figure 2. Overview of the SIICU & MATA-Former framework. (A) The SIICU pipeline transforms heterogeneous clinical records into chronological timelines, utilizing expert-verified collaborative annotation for ground truth generation. (B) The MATA-Former Architecture integrates semantic embeddings with log-transformed temporal features. The core MATA mechanism modulates self-attention via dynamic Laplacian biases, optimized against PSLs for multi-horizon risk prediction.

Figure 8. Case Study on Interpretability and Early Prediction (DIC). The top panel tracks the predicted risk score (blue) against ground truth labels (orange), highlighting a successful pre-warning window (red box). The bottom panel displays the attention weight distribution, where distinct vertical high-attention bands indicate the model’s mechanism of “locking” onto critical clinical events (Key1–Key6) and sustaining this attention over long durations. This verifies the model’s capability to capture non-contiguous, sparse dependencies in irregular clinical trajectories.
Table 5. Detailed Hyperparameter Configuration for MATA-Former.
